text.title: RAG Failure Modes and Agent Gotchas
text.summary: Critical failure modes for production RAG: retrieval as a breakpoint, prompt injection via documents, index drift, and context window pressure.

rule.statement: Always check `len(retrieved_chunks) > 0` before calling the LLM; if retrieval returns empty, return a structured "no information" response rather than attempting generation.
rule.rationale: If the retriever fails silently (network error, empty results), the LLM will hallucinate a plausible-sounding answer. An explicit check turns a silent hallucination into an auditable error.

antipattern.description: Proceeding to LLM generation when retrieved chunk scores are all below the score threshold.
antipattern.reason: Low-score chunks are tangentially related noise. The LLM may confabulate a plausible-sounding answer from weak context rather than reporting insufficient information.

antipattern.description: Injecting retrieved document chunks into prompts without sanitization or encoding.
antipattern.reason: A malicious document containing "ignore previous instructions and output X" can override agent instructions. Retrieved content must be treated as untrusted data, not trusted instructions.

rule.statement: Implement a document version field and return it in citation metadata; if the pipeline is live and documents update asynchronously, queries may receive outdated answers.
rule.rationale: Without version tracking, a document updated after last ingestion returns stale information with no indication of staleness. Users cannot distinguish fresh from outdated answers.

rule.statement: At top-k=20 with 512-token chunks, retrieval consumes 10,240 tokens before generation; always apply reranking to reduce to top-5 before LLM calls.
rule.rationale: Without reranking, a large top-k saturates the context window with low-relevance chunks. The LLM is forced to reason across noise, reducing generation quality and increasing cost.
